attributes.description: Creator deity, father of Vasishtha, produced Bálakhilyas from hair, celestial seat
Also called Mahádeva, the Great God, Lord most High, bountiful, whose banner shows pictured bull Nandi
Snow-white bull, attendant and favourite vehicle of Shiva
Divine twin children of Sun, princes compared to them
Divine beings who praised Ahalyá, came to claim dues at sacrifice
Glorious dame, wife of Gautam, freed from curse by Ráma, purified from sin and folly
Holy hermit, sage with godlike view, husband of Ahalyá, reconciled with wife, shares austerities
Mighty saint, son of Gádhi of Kusha's line, guide to Ráma, formerly warrior king, royal sage
Prince of saintly men, arch-hermit, son of Brahmá, best of those who pray, owns Dapple-skin cow
Eldest son of Gautam and Ahalyá, far-renowned, crowned with glory by austerities, filled with wonder
Dame mentioned in comparison to Ahalyá, reconciled with Bhrigu's child
Saint mentioned in comparison, reconciled with Renuka
Prince, pride of Raghu's race, lotus-eyed, freed Ahalyá, scion of King Raghu's line, strong of arm
Son of Raghu, brother of Ráma, accompanied on journey
Best of kings, high-souled, lofty-souled, planned sacrifice, ruled Mithilá
Father of Ráma and Lakshmana, king
Mighty king of earth, given birth by Lord of Life, ancestor of Vishvámitra
Son of Kusha, strong, friend of right, foe of wrong
Son of Kushanábha, father of Vishvámitra, governed land, fame undimmed by time
Heir of Gádhi, governed with kingly care, led warriors, peerless for warlike enterprise, later became hermit
Armed warriors, mad with rage, rushed on Vasishtha, burnt to ashes by sage's glance
Only surviving son of Vishvámitra, given empire, ruled as kings are taught
Divine personages of minute size, produced from hair of Brahmá, small infantry
City ruled by Janak, destination of Ráma's journey, also called Visálá
City with fair towers, another name for Mithilá
Enclosed area prepared by Janak, with thousands of Bráhmans, tents, wains
Pure abode where trees, flowers, creepers glowed, visited by saints and angels, like Brahmá's celestial seat
Mountain region visited by bards and Nágas, where Vishvámitra sought penance
Cow of spotted skin, cow of plenty, provides all needs, yields offerings, guardian and minister of Vasishtha
Gautam's decree that removed Ahalyá from sight, ended when Ráma came
Traditional offering for strangers, water for feet
Sweet, salt, pungent, bitter, acid, and astringent flavors
Fire requiring ghee/clarified butter for sacrifice
Study and teaching Veda, oblations to manes, sacrifice to Gods, hospitality, gift of food to creatures
Words like váhá, vasham pronounced at sacrifice
Wondrous knowledge with secret spell and mystic art, all arms revealed
Wondrous bow at Janak's court, virtue to be known
Ráma's presence freed Ahalyá from curse, flowers rained, nymphs and minstrels sang
Gautam received Ahalyá purified from sin, she shared in his austerities
Ráma and Lakshmana traveled with Vishvámitra to Janak's sacrifice
Noble rite prepared by King Janak, twelve days to completion, festival
Vishvámitra met Janak with chaplain Shatánanda, exchanged honors
King Vishvámitra visited hermit Vasishtha's abode with mighty army
Banquet prepared by Dapple-skin cow, fed entire army with all delicacies
Vishvámitra tried to take cow by force, cow created armies, Vasishtha defeated Vishvámitra
Vishvámitra's hundred sons burnt to ashes by Vasishtha's glance
King fled to Himálaya, performed stern penance to earn Shiva's grace
Shiva appeared to Vishvámitra, granted him science of the bow
Warriors created by cow, Persians, burning for strife, slain by Vishvámitra
Warriors created by cow, Greeks, bright and strong, in gold-hued mail
Warriors created by cow, nomad tribes from Central Asia, Scythes, fierce and dire
Warriors created by cow from her lowing, faces bright as sun
Warriors created from cow's udder, brandished spear and sword
Foreign or outcast races, created from cow's pores
Savage aborigines occupying hills and jungles, created from cow
Savage aborigines, streamed forth from cow
Wife of Rama, princess of Videha (Mithila), called the Maithil dame
Revered hermit-sage who counsels Rama and directs him to Panchavati
Mighty vulture king, old friend of Dasharatha, guardian and ally of Rama; son of Aruna; self-identifies as son of Shyeni lineage
Elder son of Aruna, brother of Jatayu
Son of Dasharatha and Kaikeyi; grieves Rama's exile and lives austerely at Ayodhya
Youngest son of Dasharatha, brother of Rama
Second queen of Dasharatha, mother of Bharata, whose plot caused Rama's exile
Queen of Dasharatha and mother of Lakshmana
Rakshasi (giantess) who encounters Rama at Panchavati; sister of Ravana (the Ten-necked tyrant)
The Ten-necked demon tyrant; brother of Surpanakha
Son of Vinata, red as the dawn; father of Sampati and Jatayu
Son of Vinata; divine eagle, swift of flight
Daughter of Daksha via Shuki-Nala lineage; wife of Kashyapa; mother of Aruna and Garuda
Divine elephant of Indra; born of Iravati (daughter of Bhadramada)
Primordial sage, son of Marichi; married eight daughters of Daksha; progenitor of gods, demons, humans, and animals
Lord of Life; father of sixty daughters, eight of whom married Kashyapa
First-born Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Second Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Third Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Fourth Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Fifth Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Sixth Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Seventh Lord of Life; father of Kashyapa
Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Lord of Life; ancestor of Ravana
Lord of Life; celestial sage
Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
Lord of Life; sun deity; praised of men
Lord of Life in the primordial genealogy
First wife of Kashyapa; mother of thirty-three gods — Vasus, Adityas, Rudras, and the twin Ashvins
Wife of Kashyapa; mother of the Daityas (demons)
Wife of Kashyapa; mother of Asvagriva
Wife of Kashyapa; mother of Narak and Kalak
Wife of Kashyapa; mother of five daughters: Kraunchi, Bhasi, Shyeni, Shuki, Dhritarashtri
Wife of Kashyapa; mother of all fruit-bearing trees
Wife of Kashyapa, swift to ire; mother of ten daughters including Mrighi, Surasa, Kadru, etc.
Wife of Kashyapa (daughter of Daksha); mother of the four human castes — Brahman, Kshatriya, Vaishya, Shudra
Daughter of Tamra; mother of cranes and owls
Daughter of Tamra; mother of water fowl
Daughter of Tamra; mother of vultures and hawks; ancestress of Jatayu's lineage
Daughter of Tamra; grandmother of Vinata via daughter Nala
Daughter of Tamra; mother of all swans, geese, and river-haunting ducks
Daughter of Shuki; mother of Vinata
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of deer
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of bears, yaks, and mountain roe
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of lions and wild monkeys
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of Iravati
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of leopards, langurs, and tigers
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of the eight directional guardian elephants
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of elephants (Matangas)
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of two celestial daughters: Gandharvi and Rohini
Daughter of Krodhavasa; mother of hooded serpents
Daughter of Krodhavasa (also called daughter of Kashyapa elsewhere); mother of countless snakes
Daughter of Bhadramada; mother of Airavata, the elephant of Indra
Daughter of Surabhi; mother of horses
Daughter of Surabhi; mother of cattle / kine
Son of Danu
Son of Kalaka
Son of Kalaka
Demon race born of Diti; their empire once spread over earth, woods, and ocean
King of the gods; owner of Airavata the divine elephant
Also called Shiva; adored by all worlds; associated with Nandi and Uma-Parvati
Also called Parvati; consort of Rudra/Shiva; daughter of Himalaya
God of love (Kamadeva); Rama's youthful grace is compared to his
Lord of the dead; the south is said to be his residence; the sun turns toward his domain in winter
Group of eight celestial gods born of Aditi
Twelve solar gods born of Aditi
Eleven storm gods born of Aditi
A star; described as a dear delight of the Moon (Lord of Night)
Forest grove four leagues from Agastya's hermitage; recommended by Agastya as Rama's dwelling; site of Surpanakha's encounter
Sacred river near Panchavati; Rama and his companions bathe here
Silver river at Ayodhya where Bharata bathes
Mountain range; described as the treasure-house of frost and cold; father of Uma/Parvati
Royal capital of Dasharatha's kingdom; where Bharata remains during Rama's exile
Kingdom of Sita's origin (Mithila); she is called the Videhan/Maithil dame
The hermitage of sage Agastya from which Panchavati is four leagues away
Rama's banishment to the forest by Dasharatha at Kaikeyi's behest; context for the entire narrative
Canto XIII — Agastya directs Rama, Lakshmana, and Sita to settle in Panchavati
Canto XIV — Rama and Lakshmana encounter Jatayu, who reveals his lineage and pledges to guard Sita
Canto XV (Panchavatí) — Lakshmana builds a cottage at Panchavati for Rama and Sita
Canto XVI — Lakshmana describes the winter season to Rama while walking to the Godavari
Canto XVII — Surpanakha, Ravana's sister, arrives at Panchavati and is captivated by Rama
Canto XIV — Jatayu narrates the primordial lineage of the Prajapatis, Kashyapa, and all creation
Virtue and righteous duty; Rama is described as 'true to virtue's path'
The four human castes born of Manu — Brahmans (mouth), Kshatriyas (chest), Vaishyas (thighs), Shudras (feet)
Divine nectar of immortality; Bharata's speech is compared to Amrita
The great Lords of Life — first-born progenitors of all creation, recited by Jatayu
The epic Sanskrit narrative from which all these verses are drawn (Cantos XIII–XVII, verses 863–882)
Vanara king, younger brother of Bali, son of Riksharajas; exiled by Bali and later restored as king of Kishkindha with Rama's help; commands the army that searches for Sita
Vanara king of Kishkindha, eldest son of Riksharajas and Indra's son; possessor of a golden chain from Indra; slain by Rama's arrow while fighting Sugreeva
Son of Bali; heir apparent; leads the southern search party for Sita; noble, brave, and installed as co-ruler by Sugreeva
Son of the Wind God (Vayu); wisest and mightiest Vanara; entrusted with Rama's ring and the mission to find Sita; leaps across the ocean to Lanka
Queen of Bali; wife who counselled Bali not to fight; her name means 'star'; later reconciled with Sugreeva
Wife of Sugreeva, taken by Bali; restored to Sugreeva after Bali's fall
Father of Bali and Sugreeva; former Vanara king
Aged and mighty bear-king; leads the southern army; rallies Hanuman to leap to Lanka
Son of Fire (Agni); Vanara chief; sent by Sugreeva to assemble armies from the northern posts
Wise Vanara physician who heals Lakshmana; Sugreeva's general who searches the west
Vanara chief in the southern search party; encourages the Vanaras to renew their toil
Fierce Vanara chief sent to lead the northern search with vast armies
Vanara chief; listed among the leaders who accompany Rama back to Ayodhya
Vanara chief; listed among the leaders on the Pushpaka Vimana return journey
Vanara warrior in Rama's allied army
First queen of Dasharatha; Rama's mother; greeted on Rama's return
Righteous brother of Ravana; defects to Rama's side; provides crucial intelligence; crowned king of Lanka after Ravana's death; owns the Pushpaka Vimana after Ravana
Chief queen of Ravana; weeps at his death; seen by Sita/Rama on the Pushpaka flight back
Son of Ravana; also called Meghanada; powerful warrior who uses serpent-arrow weapon to wound Lakshmana
Giant brother of Ravana; sleeps for months; awakened for the Lanka battle; slain by Rama
Demon chief of Janasthana; brother of Ravana; slain by Rama in Dandaka forest
Demon commander under Khara in Janasthana; killed by Rama
Three-headed demon, companion of Khara in Janasthana battle
Uncle of Ravana; takes form of the golden deer to lure Rama away from Sita; killed by Rama's arrow
Demon of Dandaka forest; first monster Rama encounters in exile; slain by Rama
Giant headless demon with long arms; killed by Rama in the forest near Janasthana; directed Rama to Sugreeva
Demon son of Dundubhi; fought with Bali for a woman's love; fled into a cave; killed by Bali; his cave episode caused Sugreeva's exile
Buffalo-demon of mountain size; challenged Ocean and Himalaya to fight; finally met and slain by Bali; his carcass was thrown a league by Bali
Holy sage whose hermitage was defiled by Bali throwing Dundubhi's carcass; cursed Bali to die if he approached Rishyamuka; his grove sheltered Sugreeva safely
Ancient sage who did 8000 seasons of penance on Vindhya hill; encountered by Sampati in his story
Ascetic woman who guards Maya's enchanted cave (the golden forest) on behalf of the apsara Hema; leads the lost Vanaras out of the cave and to the ocean shore
Chief artificer of the Danava race; built the enchanted golden cave using skills obtained by penance from Brahma; killed by Indra's thunderbolt for loving Hema
Indra's charioteer; descends from heaven to lend Indra's divine chariot to Rama for the final battle with Ravana
Son of Sampati; brings Sampati food and reports seeing Ravana carrying Sita southward over the ocean
Creator God; granted Maya the boon of knowledge; the Pushpaka Vimana is described as fashioned by his creative mind; dwells beyond the northern ocean
Preserver God; his three strides symbolising the rising, zenith, and setting of the sun are referenced in the description of the eastern mountain (Jatarupam)
God of the sea; his realm is the ocean; Sampati asks to be led to offer libations at the sea (Varuna's home)
God of Wealth; brother of Ravana (son of Vishravas); his celestial palace on Kailasa is described in the northern search canto; the Pushpaka Vimana was originally his, seized by Ravana
Wind God; father of Hanuman; Hanuman's might and speed are attributed to his divine parentage
Sun God (Vivasvat); burns Sampati's wings in the myth of the brothers' flight; Agastya's hymn to Surya (Aditya Hridayam) is recited before the final battle
Also called Sesha; cosmic serpent with a thousand heads; supports the earth; described in the eastern search canto resting on Mount Jatarupam; gods bow to him
Nymphs of Paradise; play in Kubera's lotus pools on Kailasa; Hema is an apsara for whom Maya's cave was built
Celestial nymph (apsara); for whose love Maya built the enchanted cave; after Indra killed Maya, Brahma bestowed the cave to Hema
Mountain city and capital of the Vanara kingdom; Bali's royal town; Sugreeva is restored as its king after Bali's death
Hill near Matanga's hermitage; Sugreeva lives here in exile because Matanga's curse prevents Bali from approaching; Rama first meets Sugreeva here
Hill near Kishkindha where Rama and Lakshmana spend the rainy season while waiting for Sugreeva to act on his promise
Mountain (possibly Malaya range) where Rama observes the onset of the rains and mourns Sita
Major mountain range in central India; the southern search army passes through it; Sampati falls on Vindhya after his wings are burnt
Mountain on the eastern coast; Hanuman takes his great leap to Lanka from Mahendra's peak; Sampati's son Suparsva stood on Mahendra and saw Ravana carrying Sita
Island city of Ravana; situated on Trikuta mountain surrounded by the southern ocean; built by Vishwakarma; captured after the great battle
Three-peaked mountain on which Lanka is enthroned; seen from the Pushpaka Vimana on Rama's return journey
Village where Bharata waits in austerity with Rama's sandals on the throne; from which Bharata goes out to meet Rama
Forest where Rama spent exile; Viradha, Surpanakha encounter, Jatayu's death all occur here
Part of Dandaka forest; Khara's base; where Jatayu died fighting Ravana
Lotus-covered lake near Rishyamuka where Rama mourns Sita in the melancholy season
Mountain of Shiva; also site of Kubera's palace; described in the northern search canto
Cosmic golden mountain at the centre of Jambudvipa; Vishnu's second step rests on its crest; 84,000 yojanas high
River near Ayodhya; the Pushpaka crosses it as it returns
River (modern Nerbudda) in the southern search route; Sugreeva directs the search army through its valley
Magical underground forest of golden trees and silver streams built by the Danava architect Maya; guarded by Svayamprabha; the Vanaras wander in it for a month
The central continent; contains Meru at its centre; referenced in the description of the cosmic geography during the eastern search
Blessed northern land of the holy spirits; described in the northern search canto as a paradise of golden lilies, pearl-decked maidens, and jewelled streams
Canto IX — Sugreeva narrates how Bali drove him from the kingdom after mistakenly believing Sugreeva had usurped the throne while Bali was in Mayavi's cave
Canto XI — Bali kills the buffalo-demon Dundubhi; the carcass thrown near Matanga's hermitage; Matanga's curse prevents Bali from entering Rishyamuka
Canto XII — Rama pierces seven palm trees with one arrow to prove his might to the doubting Sugreeva; the arrow passes through six subterranean realms and returns to the quiver
Alliance forged between Rama and Sugreeva: Rama will slay Bali; Sugreeva will help find Sita

nodes.name: First Battle of Bali and Sugreeva

attributes.description: Canto XII — Sugreeva challenges Bali; Rama cannot shoot because the brothers look identical; Sugreeva retreats to Rishyamuka wounded
Canto XVI — Rama's arrow pierces Bali's chest while Bali fights Sugreeva (wearing a garland to distinguish him); Bali delivers his famous speech before dying
Canto XVII — Dying Bali rebukes Rama for shooting from hiding; Rama explains the justice of the act; Bali is reconciled and dies attaining Brahma's worlds
Canto XXVI — Sugreeva is anointed king of Kishkindha with full Aryan coronation rites; Angad is made co-ruler; Rama cannot enter the city due to his 14-year forest vow
Cantos XXVII-XXVIII — Rama and Lakshmana spend the four-month rainy season in a cave on Prasravana hill; Rama mourns Sita
Canto XXIX — Hanuman rebukes the pleasure-absorbed Sugreeva and urges him to repay Rama's help by launching the search
Cantos XL-XLIII — Sugreeva sends four armies to the east, south, west, and north to search for Sita; describes cosmic geography of all four directions
Canto XLIV — Rama gives Hanuman his signet ring as a token for Sita; gives special counsel to Hanuman above all others
Cantos LVI-LX — Sampati, brother of Jatayu, reveals Sita's location in Ravana's Lanka after hearing of Jatayu's death; his son Suparsva had seen Ravana carrying Sita
Cantos L-LII — The southern army stumbles into Maya's magic cave, wanders for a month; Svayamprabha feeds them and miraculously leads them out
Hanuman takes his great leap of 100 leagues across the ocean from Mahendra's peak to Lanka; passes through sky, meets obstacles
Hanuman locates Sita imprisoned in Ravana's Ashoka grove in Lanka; gives her Rama's ring; receives Chudamani jewel for Rama
After Ravana's court burns Hanuman's tail, Hanuman sets Lanka ablaze before returning to report to Rama
Nala (son of Vishwakarma) architects a bridge 100 leagues long across the ocean from the Indian shore to Lanka; the Vanara armies cross it
The great battle between Rama's Vanara-bear army and Ravana's demon forces at Lanka; spanning many cantos; includes deaths of Indrajit, Kumbhakarna, and finally Ravana
Canto CII — Lakshmana is gravely wounded by a serpent-weapon; Hanuman flies to Mahodaya/Himalaya, uproots the entire peak carrying healing herbs; Susena heals Lakshmana
Canto CIII — Indra sends his divine chariot with Matali as charioteer so Rama can fight Ravana on equal footing from a chariot
Canto CVI — Agastya appears on the battlefield and recites the Aditya Hridayam (hymn to the Sun) to Rama, infusing him with strength for the final duel with Ravana
Rama slays the ten-headed Ravana with the divine Brahmastra arrow described by Agastya; the world is freed from the demon's tyranny
Cantos CXXIII-CXXV — Vibhishana gives the divine chariot Pushpaka to Rama; Rama, Sita, Lakshmana, Sugreeva, Vibhishana, and their allies fly back to Ayodhya; Rama points out landmarks below
Canto CXXIX — Rama lands at Nandigrama; emotional reunion with Bharata who has kept the sandals; Rama is joyfully welcomed back to Ayodhya
Divine flying chariot originally owned by Kubera, seized by Ravana; gifted by Vibhishana to Rama after the victory; carries Rama's entire party back to Ayodhya
Bridge built by Nala across 100 leagues of ocean from the Indian coast to Lanka; sacred to all worlds for all time; Hanuman rests on Hiranyanabha/Mainaka island midway
Token given by Rama to Hanuman as proof of identity for Sita; bears Rama's name; Sita recognises it and trusts Hanuman
Golden chain given to Bali by his father Indra/Mahendra as a divine gift; preserved Bali's life even as he lay wounded; still gleaming as he died
Supreme divine weapon used by Rama to slay Ravana in the final duel; its impact is described as like Yama's discus ending the world
Royal coronation rite performed for Sugreeva at Kishkindha and anticipated for Rama at Ayodhya; involves sacred water poured by Vanara chiefs
Fourteen-year forest exile of Rama; the vow that prevents him from entering cities during this period; context for the entire Kishkindha and Lanka episodes
Sacred hymn to the Sun recited by Agastya to Rama before the final battle; embedded within the Ramayana
Third of the four Vedas; its sacred chants begin in the Sravana/rainy month; mentioned in the description of the rainy season
Supreme deity void of all fear, compared to Ráma's fearlessness
Light's Queen clings to Sun as Ráma to Sítá, swords rival sun in sheen
Goddess of wealth and prosperity, love shown in Ráma's bright abode
Fire God, worship due to him shown to Suyajña
Force whose favor must be won through duty
Son of Vasishtha, of Bráhman race, first and holiest one, wise, knew Scripture, found in hall of Fire, performed noonday rites
Best of Bráhmans, likely Vishvámitra, to receive gifts
Deep in Scripture, serves Kausalyá with blessing and respect, true to queen
Tuneful bard and charioteer, true and dear, ancient friend and minister of Ráma
Numerous band who go with staff in hand, trained, prize deep study, love dainty fare, praised by good
Aged Bráhman born of Garga's race, long in evil case, earned living with spade and plough, poor with many children
Young wife who bore babes, felt indigence, counseled husband to seek Ráma
Divine Prajápati first created by Brahmá, bright with saintly light
Woman who gave all to Satyaván in heart/body/soul/mind, exemplar of wifely devotion
Son of Dyumatsena, husband to whom Sávitrí was devoted
Father of Satyaván
Father of Kaikeyí
Wilderness full of woes: snakes, scorpions, flies, thorny trees, tangled grass, where elephant and roebuck roam, pathless wild, lonely shade, greenwood
Bright dwelling rich in Lakshmí's love, five courts, where treasures distributed, where Sítá prepared to give wealth
Teacher's hall where celestial arms preserved with care
Chapel where sacred fire used in worship is kept, where Suyajña found
River with banks where herds of kine fed, farther bank where Trijata's staff landed
Dwelling in grove where cows driven, home of poor Bráhman
Homes of Gods, celestial sphere ruled by Indra, worlds where devoted sons win place, Brahmá's glorious sphere
Woes of wilderness: snakes, scorpions, grasshoppers, flies, thorny bushes, tangled grass, fatigue, countless fears, grief and pain
Penance with every thought, no fear of things to fear, forever drear, coat of bark and matted hair, roots/fruit/honey as food
Woman follows husband as Sávitrí followed Satyaván, wife committed to husband in heart/body/soul/mind, husband is God
Truth-declaring Bráhmans foretold Sítá's fortune to dwell in wood, knew future by marks and signs
Ancient teaching that woman given by parents with holy rites is wife in this world and after life
Supreme duty to obey sire and mother, triple world comprises father/mother/holy guide, sonly love and reverence storms heaven
Two fearful bows given by Varuna to Janak, sword-proof mail, quivers with arrows that never fail, golden-hilted swords keen as sun
Gold, silver, gems, pearls, necklaces, girdles, bracelets, beds, elephants, thousand coins, kine given to Bráhmans and servants
Worn by those who wait on Kausalyá with care, mark of Bráhman status
Ráma described perils: snakes, scorpions, thorny trees, hope/anger cast aside, penance required, deemed Sítá unfit
Lady with deep distress begged to accompany, cited love, father's command, Bráhmans' prophecy, threatened death if refused
Daughter of Videha's king accused Ráma of being woman in disguise, likened to low actor, spirit tried by fear/anger/love/pride
Hero's heart reconciled to take Sítá to Dandaka, embraced wife, sweet words bid her wake to life, approved her going
Brother pressed Ráma's feet with fervor, begged to follow to woods with bow, would go before, carry basket/spade, guard sleeping
Commanded brother to bid adieu, bring celestial bows from preceptor's hall, summon Suyajña, bestow wealth on Bráhmans
Ráma honored Suyajña with gems/pearls, gave gifts to Agastya/Kushika's son/Bráhmans/servants, distributed gold/silver/cows/elephants
Poor aged Bráhman came to fifth court, described poverty, Ráma tested with staff-throw, gave thousand kine and wealth
Sítá and princes with celestial arms went to monarch's house, crowds gathered on houses/terraces mourning
Deity to whom oblations and offerings are burnt
Free spirit pervading all life, God who roves at will, wooed hundred daughters, bent them when rejected
Forest spirits addressed by Vishvámitra at Perfect Grove
Devoutest sage, most continent, glorious saint absorbed in penance, spiritual father of Brahmadatta
Heavenly maid, child of Urmilá, served Chúli, mother of Brahmadatta, lived unwed maiden life
Great sage, husband of Satyavatí
Elder sister of Vishvámitra, pure and good, married to Richíka, became celestial river Kaushikí
Rover of the Night, demon, man-eater foul of mind, struck by Ráma's arrow, hurled hundred leagues into ocean
Rover of the Night, demon who showered blood, killed by Ráma's flaming arrow
Fiends accompanying Márícha and Suváhu, disturbers of holy rites, overthrown by Ráma
Prince of high renown, son of Kusha, builder of Kaushambí town
Son of Kusha, dwelt in Dharmáranya's citadel
Son of Kusha, built city Girivraja, ruled fertile Magadh's broad champaign
Spiritual son of Chúli, rich and great king, ruled Kámpilí, married hundred daughters of Kushanábha
Pious monarch of Ayodhyá in days of yore, childless, longed for children
Fair-faced consort of Sagar, sprang from Vidarbha's royal race, famed for truth from youth
King mentioned in context, likely ruler referenced
Daughters of Kushanábha born to Queen Ghritáchí, lovely-faced with every charm, bent by Wind God, married to Brahmadatta
Nymph most fair, queen married to Kushanábha, mother of hundred daughters
Elder daughter of Himálaya and Mená, three-pathed river, purges sinners, cleanse and save, Rivers' Queen, also called Jáhnaví
Younger daughter of Himálaya, passed long time in vows and rigid fast, became Rudra's bride, supreme among Goddesses
Mother of Gangá and Umá, offspring of will of Meru, Himálaya's darling, beauty of dainty waist
Consecrated wood, holy hermitage, perfect seat, dwelling of hermits, scene of Vishvámitra's sacrifice
Fertile land ruled by King Janak, location of planned sacrifice, where wondrous bow is kept
Beneath Himálaya's snowy peaks, destination of Vishvámitra's journey
River where travelers halted, distant shore, also called Hiranyaráhu/Hiranyaráha (Golden-armed/Auriferous)
Town built by Kushámba
City with high towers raised by Kushanábha, another name of Kanyakubja
Citadel where Amúrtarajas chose to dwell, wood to which God of Justice fled
City built by Vasu, in Magadh neighbourhood
Vasu's old domain, fertile broad champaign with smiling fields adorned with golden corn
Lovely rill bursting from parent hill, flows between mountains like wreath, roves through Magadh's plains
City of Bent Virgins, modern Kanauj/Canouge, royal town named after Wind God bent maidens
Fortunate metropolis where King Brahmadatta maintained his state
World of endless light where Kusha vanished to
River with distant shore, pleasant shore where night was passed
Another name for River Gangá, hermit-haunted flood, Rivers' Queen, daughter of Jahnu

Law ordained of old for giants when days are told, bodies laid in earth they rise to homes eternal in skies

Entertained passed night, with morning's light bade adieu, pierced mighty forest where roamed deer pard bear, ruined pools scarce seen, creeper rent prostrate tree
Deep in thickets stood, saw giant passing nature's law, vast as mountain, hideous, wore tiger's hide, carried beasts on iron spear, charged with roar and yell

Saw Virádha clasp Sítá, cried to brother with pale lips terror dried, Kaikeyí's blow falls, cruel soul triumphant, bitterest grief another touch Maithil dame

Each warrior drew sword black as serpent, gathering fury rushed on giant, giant cast arm around each held heroes fast, bearing twain turned and fled
Giant upraised them to lofty shoulders, like children clung to neck, rover of night bore princes through wood vast as cloud, where birds flew trees threw shadows
Viewed with wild affright heroes hurried from sight, tossed shapely arms high, shrieked bitter cry, begged take me leave Raghu's sons free
Heroes heard mournful cry, hastened for lady's sake, Lakshmana broke foe's left arm, Ráma smashed right with heavy hand, giant came to earth fainting
Rushed on crushed and beat with arms fists feet, nerved mighty limb to pound and bray on ground, arrows and blades made wide rents, crushed torn mangled still lived could not kill
Ráma saw no arms might slay charmed life, gave counsel dig pit to fit elephantine bulk, let hollowed earth enfold giant, pressed foot upon breast

Princes rejoiced to see him die, strayed in boundless wood like great sun and moon triumphant in sky

Offerings to spirits/shades to be paid
Ráma broke heavenly bow in two, awful clang like tempest, earth shook, people fell senseless

attributes.context: Janak's ministers traveled three nights to Ayodhyá bearing news

attributes.description: King traveled four days with quadruple host, priests, to Videha
Younger brother summoned from Sánkáshyá to share in bliss
Vasishtha claimed four daughters for four princes, Janak agreed
Janak fought and slew Sudhanvá who demanded Shiva's bow and Sítá
Fierce hot warfare with Haihayas, Tálajanghas, Shashivindus; Asit forced to flee
Kálindi bore son conceived by Chyavan, born with poison, named Sagar
Four princes to marry four maidens on same lucky day in Phálguni
Uncle came seeking to see nephew Bharat, bearing message from Kekaya
Dasharatha gave 400,000 cows, one hundred thousand for each prince
God who forms and fashions all, prayed to aid hospitable feast, art raised amazing structures
Moon deity called to give noblest food to feed multitude
Celestial minstrel called by hermit to feast
Celestial minstrel called by hermit
Sweet celestial minstrel called to feast
Apsaras of beauty rare, fairest maid who danced to Bharat
Apsaras with distinctive tangled hair
Fair Apsaras
Apsaras sweet to view
Apsaras sweet to view
Lovely Apsaras
Apsaras bright to view who danced
Apsaras who danced
Apsaras who danced
Gandharva who came
Gandharva like radiant flame
Household officers, sat by degrees from chief to least
Captain of host and honoured men
Tree which lent shade, grass laid beneath, where Ráma and Sítá spent night together ere they went
River bank where Raghu's son lay till break of day, army crossed pure wave
Town of Guha, lord of Nishádas, from where boats prepared
Famous wood where army stood in auspicious hour after crossing Gangá
Holy dwelling, best of saints' abode, hermit's grove, holy shade, chapel where sacred fire burns
Wilderness where Ráma roams in banishment
Happy land in remote north, inhabitants enjoy natural perfection with complete happiness, no vicissitude/death/fear, Kuvera's garden lies there
Blissful celestial garden, shade of trees where Gods take ease, twenty thousand maids came from
Celestial gardens whose chaplets bloomed in Prayága's shade
Scented mountain from which delicious cooling gales came
Scented mountain from which gales came throwing auspicious fragrance
Three paid with ruled lips devoutly staid after sipping water
Lakshmana kept watch all night as sentinel, bow strung, hand gloved, arm braced, quivers hung
Sacred grass heaps, tumbled where Ráma and Sítá lay, pleasant bed spread by Lakshmana, stamped with signs of limbs
Saw bed, lion-lord on bare earth, sudden fall from bliss, senses distraught like dream, desolate fate
From this hour nights pass on earth/grass, eat only fruit/roots, wear bark coat and matted hair, remain for Ráma's term
Five hundred boats moored, mystic signs raised, mighty bells hung, some swam with pitchers, rafts, arms
Mighty banquet, hospitable entertainment, magical celestial feast with divine food, drink, dancers, musicians
Art raised structures: five leagues smooth ground, mansions with halls, palace high as cloud, throne, trees blooming
Hermit prayed to Gods, minstrels, rivers, Apsarases, all came in manifest semblance
Army delirious with delight, soldiers cried ne'er return to Ayodhyá, exulting in lawless glee, heaven for perfect bliss
Three paid evening worship, Lakshmana spread grass bed, Ráma and Sítá lay reclining, Lakshmana kept vigil all night
Bharat with friends and mothers saw tree and bed, beheld tumbled grass, particles of gold, silken threads, lamented
Raghu's son woke with earliest light, spoke to Shatrughna, Guha came with kindly questioning
Guha sped to town, told kinsmen, five hundred boats ready moored, mystic signs raised, bells hung
Army boarded boats, Bharat/Shatrughna/queens/priest/elders first, boats manned by Guha's servants, swimming elephants appeared, all crossed
Army stood in famous wood in auspicious hour, prince addressed weary men to rest
Prince saw dwelling league away, left multitude, went on foot with two linen robes, Vasishtha walked before
Hermit saw Vasishtha drawing near, gave grace-gift water and fruit, questioned in order, Bharat paid reverence due
Saint asked why Bharat came, if to harm Ráma, enjoy realm without thorn, Bharat sobbed denied
Hermit pressed by Vasishtha and priests replied graciously, granted prayer, told Chitrakúta location, urged stay for feast
Hermit prayed to Vishvakarma, three world-guards, streams, minstrels, Apsarases, all came in manifest semblance
Delicious gales blew, immortal flowers fell, heavenly drums sounded, Apsarases danced, minstrels sang
Vishvakarma's art raised structures: five leagues level ground, fruit trees, streams, mansions, palace high as cloud
Army amazed and delighted, twenty thousand nymphs served, Gandharvas sang, danced, all fed with celestial food
Army spent night in joy, entranced, soldiers cried to stay forever, exulting in lawless glee, seemed like dream
Minstrels/streams/dames returned, Bharat with morning's light drew near hermit, questioned if night content
The royal household priest and chief counselor who directs the envoys to summon Bharat from Rájagriha.
A renowned Bráhman sage who attends the assembly after Dasharatha's death.
An ancient revered sage present at the assembly of Bráhmans in Ayodhyá.
An innocent young ascetic accidentally shot by Dasharatha while he was filling a water-pot at the Sarju river.
The preserver of the universe, invoked in Kaushalya's blessing; Ráma is compared to his power.
The God of cosmic waters and moral order, invoked in blessings for Ráma's protection.
The God of rain, mentioned in the discourse on the necessity of a king for prosperous lands.
The regent of the planet Venus, invoked for Ráma's protection in the forest.
The dense forest wilderness where Ráma, Sítá, and LakshmaG are exiled for fourteen years.
The river on whose bank Dasharatha accidentally shot the hermit's son, an event that cursed his fate.
The city of Bharat's maternal grandfather A[vapati, where Bharat and Zatrughna reside during the exile.
The sacred confluence of Gangá and Yamuná rivers, near Bharadvája's hermitage; modern Allahabad.
The prosperous kingdom ruled by Dasharatha with Ayodhyá as its capital.
The 14-year banishment of Ráma to the DaGak forest, initiated by Kaikeyí's boon from Dasharatha.
The death of King Dasharatha from grief following Ráma's departure into exile.
The ceremonial blessing and rites performed by Queen Kaushalya for Ráma before his departure.
The formal leave-taking of Ráma, Sítá, and LakshmaG from Ayodhyá in Sumantra's chariot.
Sumantra's return to Ayodhyá alone after leaving Ráma at the forest, conveying Ráma's final message.
Dasharatha's accidental killing of a hermit's son on the Sarju bank while hunting by sound in his youth.
The council of Bráhmans and ministers who met after Dasharatha's death to decide on installing a king.
Vaishtha's dispatch of fast messengers to summon Bharat back to Ayodhyá after the king's death.
Ráma's first night halt under the Ingudí tree on the Gangá bank during the forest journey.
Ráma and LakshmaG's arrival at the sage Bharadvája's hermitage near Prayag during the exile journey.
The obligation of a son to honor and obey his parents, central to Ráma's acceptance of exile.
The concept of righteous rule; the assembly's discourse highlights the necessity of a king for justice and prosperity.
The keeping of sworn words; Dasharatha's oath to Kaikeyí and Ráma's own pledge drive key events.
Banishment to the forest as a form of renunciation and testing, undertaken by Ráma for 14 years.
A canto in which the Bráhmans lament the dangers of a kingless land after Dasharatha's death.
The canto in which Vaishtha sends swift messengers to bring Bharat back from Rájagriha.
The canto in which Dasharatha confesses to Kaushalya his past sin of accidentally killing a hermit's son.
The canto recording Queen Kaushalya's elaborate ritual blessing of Ráma before his departure into exile.
The canto where Sítá successfully persuades Ráma to allow her to accompany him into the forest.
The canto describing the formal departure of Ráma, Sítá, and LakshmaG from Ayodhyá.

nodes.name: The Halt Under The Ingudí (Canto L)

attributes.description: The canto in which Ráma rests at the Gangá bank under the Ingudí tree and meets the Nisháda king Guha.
The canto in which LakshmaG laments the suffering caused by Ráma's exile while keeping night watch.
The canto describing Ráma's visit to the sage Bharadvája at the confluence of Gangá and Yamuná.
God whose hands wield thunder, invoked to guard east
Guards of hoary age held in honor, sat at portal with many more, sprang to feet with acclamation
Stationed to keep doors, flew to chamber to bear tidings to Kausalyá
Women in palace who wailed and lamented at Ráma's exile, compared to kine bereaved of calves
Crowd of good and sage masters of Veda text, dear to king for lore and age, venerate majesty and royal state
Lunar asterism, favorite wife of Moon, compared to Sítá blessed with Ráma's love
Lord of Cooling Rays, retains sweet splendor in time of wane, King of Rivers waits for darling moon
Demons who fought Gods in ancient war when Dasharatha saved Kaikeyí
Dasharatha's dwelling with lofty halls, ladies' bower, three broad courts with bowmen, chambers with lattice windows, central court
Queen's dwelling with splendid bower, portal guarded by warders, multiple courts, where offerings made
Women's quarters where Dasharatha sat with Kaikeyí, where sentence pronounced
Path traversed by Ráma, filled with incense of aloe, high palaces like pale clouds, flowers of myriad colors, food for varied taste
Where multitude stood watching in street expecting Ráma's return
Imperial rite to join Ráma in royal sway, sanctifies ruler's might, lustral rites and fast, when moon and Pushya meet, all royal pomp
Coat of bark and matted hair, black deer's skin and roebuck's horn, hermit dress
First and paramount duty that sons obediently fulfill father's word and will, father as deity on earth
Sacrificial elements: oil, grain, vases, wreaths, curds, cates, wood, milk, sesamum, rice, poured in sacred fire
Imperial sway and governance to be yielded to Bharat by king's decree
Roots, fruit, honey, abstaining from flesh, living in solitude
Sumantra brought message that king and Kaikeyí seek interview with Ráma
Hero rode lofty car like fire, coursers swift, Lakshmana behind, crowds following, women throwing chaplets, benedictions
Ráma found father with hopeless eye, pallid mien, voice choked, prostrate, could not speak or look
Ruthless dame revealed two boons: Bharat to throne, Ráma to exile for 14 years in Dandaka
Noble youth calm and unmoved, promised to fulfill father's word, go to forest, yield empire to Bharat
Battle when Dasharatha fought transfixed with darts, bathed in gore, saved by Kaikeyí who earned two boons
Hero left ladies' bower, dismissed retinue, refused chouries and umbrella, sought mother's mansion
Ráma came to mother's splendid bower, she fed sacred flame, made offerings, embraced and blessed son
Mother wore white, fasted, kept vigil, made offerings to Vishnu and Lakshmí, awaited son's consecration
Consort of Indra, also called Shachí and Indrání
Mighty monkey, offspring of Vrihaspati, matchless chieftain boast for wisdom of Vánar host
Brave and bold monkey, son of Lord of Gold
Monkey, son of Ashvins, famed for beauty like sires
Monkey, son of Varuna
Monkey, son of rain god Parjanya
Well-honoured sage, hermit of noble mind, guided king's rites, married to Shántá, son of Kashyapa/Vibhándak
Daughter of Dasharatha, wife of Rishyasring, Kshatriya who married Bráhman, devoted to husband
Hermit, father of Rishyasring, son of Kashyapa, of Bráhmans first and best
Vast being of awful might from sacrificial flame, dark robed in red, brought golden vase of nectar
Realm of Gods where nectar is quaffed, home of celestial beings
Mortal world where Vishnu takes birth, domain guarded by Ráma for ten thousand years
Town of Lomapád, wreathed with Champacs' leafy crown, where Rishyasring returned
Woodland dwelling of Rishyasring and Shántá, where they returned after rites
Indra's heaven where Ashvins serve as physicians
Horse Sacrifice, rite that wins an heir, performed by Dasharatha
Divine drink/Amrit brought in golden vase, gives sons and wealth, blessed by Gods
Monkey-formed beings created by Gods as helps for Vishnu, skilled in war, change form at will
Spiritual practice through which Rávana gained favor from Brahmá
First month of spring, corresponds to latter half March and former half April
Seventh lunar mansion, presided over by Aditi
Ninth lunar mansion called the Snake
Zodiacal sign, the Crab, where sun was when twins were born
Zodiacal sign Mina (fish), under which Bharat was born
Constellation containing stars in wing of Pegasus, four-fold light
Walking round object keeping right side towards it, mark of great respect
Rite freeing from stain, performed for Rishyasring's inter-caste marriage
Celestial musicians inhabiting Indra's heaven, orchestra at divine banquets
Demigods attendant on Kuvera, employed in care of garden and treasures
Demigods serving Kuvera, celestial musicians, like centaurs with human figures and horses' heads
Demigods/spirits occupying middle air between earth and sun
Gods pray to Nárāyana/Vishnu to take human birth and slay Rávana
Vishnu agrees to take mortal birth, divide being in four, reign 10,000 years
Ashvamedha rite performed by childless king to win an heir
Vast being emerges from sacrificial flame bearing golden vase of nectar
King gives divine drink to three queens: Kausalyá (half), Kaikeyí (half residue), Sumitrá (twice)
Brahmá commands Gods to create monkey-formed helpers for Vishnu's incarnation
Gods, sages, celestial beings beget sons in monkey forms with divine powers
Kausalyá bears Ráma on Chaitra's ninth day, moon in Punarvasu, five planets at apex
Kaikeyí bears Bharat under Pushya mansion and Pisces sign
Sumitrá bears Lakshmana and Shatrughna when sun reached Cancer, moon with Snake
Sage returns with Shántá after completing Dasharatha's rites
Sage and Shántá depart from Ayodhyá back to hermitage
Kashyapa's son travels to city to see his son Rishyasring
Vibhándak performs ceremony to free Rishyasring from stain of inter-caste marriage
Nymphs danced, minstrels sang, heavenly drums pealed, flowers rained at princes' births
Deities of particular class with five or ten enumerated, worshipped at funeral obsequies for deceased progenitors
Celestial beings who assemble at Brahmá's call, advance in shining train
Cot built by Lakshmana with timber from wood, firmly built, roofed with leaves, fair to see, well-set, fenced from wind and rain
Mountain beneath whose peak woods hang where Ashvins seek
Sacrifice paid for lengthened life with antelope venison, as Scripture bids, verses repeated to make complete
Ritual with muttered prayer, water, grass, wood, fire, offerings to Gods and shades, bathed duly in river, good offerings remove sin
Many built to suit holy shade, decked with woodland chaplets, fruit, roots, roasted meat
Joining hands, head bent, greeting sent to father's feet, mother's blessing, honor to king like Gods, duty to monarchs paramount
Soul on fire, breathing fast words of ire, king is cause/poor slave, questioned king's authority, ties loosed
Locks wound in votive fashion, coats of bark bound
Trees hang low, shoot/bud/flower dead, floods dried, groves drear, beasts still, serpents don't crawl, lethargy of woe, blossoms lost scent
Elaborate metaphor: sorrow raging sea, Ráma's absence swells tide, Sítá's absence makes wide, tears foam, sighs billows, cries roar, Kaikeyí fire beneath
Persuasive art, soothing fancies, pleasant words to cheer, conduct buys wide glory
Cannot live single hour without them, needs must go to forest or Yama's realm, shrill lament 'My son, my son!'
Ráma, Lakshmana, Sítá spoke raising suppliant hands to anchoret, requested to stay, saint entertained with honor
Lakshmana brought timber, built leafy hut deftly with branches, firmly built and walled with wood
Sacrifice paid with antelope venison, Lakshmana shot deer, meat broiled, sacred rites begun, Ráma repeated verses, celestial hosts came
Paid Vishvedevas, Rudra, Vishnu due, wonted blessings, made altars and shrines, entered pleasant cot, forgot exile's woe
Guha's heart sank, spent with sorrow, yoked steeds, sped to Ayodhyá, third day reached gate, city drowned in sorrow
Hundreds/thousands ran crying 'Where is Ráma?', drew deep sighs, wailed, tears fell when knew he crossed flood
Sumantra through seven courts, royal ladies flocked from terraces, heard them mourn for Ráma, reached monarch's hall
King heard message, heart well-nigh broke, fainting fell to earth, senses astray, overcome by grief
With Sumitrá raised lord dismayed, cried to him, lay prostrate, voice drowned by sobs, flocked around and wept
Sumantra told greeting to father's feet, mother's blessing, honor king like Gods, message to Bharat, Lakshmana's angry words
Locks in votive coils, bark coats, crossed Gangá, went to Prayág, Lakshmana walked ahead, forced to return

attributes.context: Realms mourn, trees withering, floods dried, groves drear, beasts still, wood silent, crushed with grief

attributes.description: Trembling, lying prostrate, half awoke, begged to be borne to forest or Yama's realm, no power to linger
Choked by tears, suppliant hands, urged dismiss grief, described Sítá's contentment, Ráma's protection, conduct buys glory
Sun deity whose rays consume parching plain in summer's noontide blaze
Will share glories with Sítá and Earth awaiting heir
Led monarch to Kausalyá's bower, laid him with reverential care
Saintly twice-born, triply old in glory/knowledge/seasons, hoary heads bowed, walked with Ráma, wailed with wild lament
Followed Ráma, felt fond affection, wept, lay around on grassy shore, sleeping crowd, citizens who roam, devoted
Queen's chamber where warders laid Dasharatha with reverential care
Wilderness where Ráma sent, unpeopled, tenants creep to lair/den/nest, hermit-haunted wood, desert place, dark with lovely trees
River with eddying flood and fast waves, grassy shore, fringed with grassy mead, where Ráma halted first night
Ancestral dwelling from which citizens far roam
Exile to wilderness, forest life, fourteen years in wild, hermit's garb, sent to abide by woe untried
King's soul disquieted, tossed arms lamenting, sight went with Ráma, choking sobs, voice half spent, piteous cry
Mother's bitter grief, years of woe, tears of anguish, quenchless flames burn, compared to lioness robbed
Kausalyá believes she dried udders of kine, left calves to pine, hence childless now
Righteous words, hopeful argument, soothing eloquent speech, grief began to leave
Fond affection, followed Ráma, would not consent to quit lives, never leave firm intent, care of house resigned
Keeps vows firm and sure, duty makes spirit pure, noble gifts abound, decked with all grace, filial duty path
Sacred fires borne on aged shoulders following to woods, Vájapeya rites canopies, Vedic lore deep in bosoms
Ráma took only water to slake thirst on first night, varied store wood provides
Ráma left while people slept, drove north then back to mislead, so citizens wouldn't follow
Woke to find no Ráma, fear and grief subdued senses, contemplated death by fire, pilgrimage to Himálayas
City wore no beauty, dull river, moonless sky, dry sea, all joy fled, no trader showed wares, mothers scarce smiled
Wives assailed returning lords with piercing words like steel, questioned worth of home/sons/wealth without Ráma
Pools/fountains made holy by Ráma's use, forests yearn to please, mountains welcome, trees fling wealth before him
Hated gloom fell on Dasharatha, at midnight cried for Kausalyá, sight went with Ráma
Queen addressed lord on bed, accused Kaikeyí of venom, mourned son's banishment, questioned former life sins
Fair queen gave righteous answer, praised Ráma's virtues, prophesied joyful return, soothed Kausalyá's heart
People followed Ráma, king turned back, citizens still tracked chariot, reached delightful river place
Hero spoke to cheer like own children, urged them to love Bharat, praised Bharat's virtues, people longed for his empire
Twice-born wailed, raised hoary heads, called on horses to return, walked with Ráma, bore worship fires
Sun set at Tamasá, Sumantra tended horses, Ráma took only water, laid on leafy bed, Lakshmana kept watch all night
Ráma rose, saw sleeping crowd, commanded swift journey while unconscious, drove north then back to mislead
Morn shone, people found no Ráma, fear and grief subdued senses, searched every place, contemplated death
Each mansion filled with wailing, wives assailed returning lords, praised Lakshmana's loyalty, blessed nature Ráma touches
A compassionate Rakshasa matron who dreams of Lanka's fall and comforts Sita.
An old and wise Rakshasa counselor who advises Ravana to make peace with Rama.
A friendly Rakshasa woman who consoles Sita during her captivity.
A spy sent by Ravana to inspect the Vanara army, captured and released by Rama's forces.
Another spy of Ravana who describes the main Vanara chiefs to his king.
The mountain near Lanka from which Rama and the Vanaras survey the enemy city.
The great sea separating India and Lanka, bridged by the Vanara army.
Rama's ring delivered by Hanuman to Sita as a token of identity.
Sita's crest jewel given to Hanuman to deliver to Rama.
A deceptive illusion of Rama's severed head created by Ravana's magician to grieve Sita.
A prophetic dream indicating the victory of Rama and the destruction of Lanka.
Vibhishana defecting from Ravana and allying with Rama.
The construction of the bridge (Setu) by Nala and Neela to cross over to Lanka.
The legendary sage credited with compiling the Vedas and Puranas.
A devotee of Vishnu who attained the status of the pole star.
The genre of 18 major Hindu encyclopedic scriptures.
The supreme goddess, representing cosmic energy and female divinity.
Ritual fire sacrifices and offerings.
Cosmic age cyclical divisions (Satya, Treta, Dvapara, Kali).
The elephant-headed deity of wisdom and remover of obstacles.
The god of war, son of Shiva and Parvati.
The cosmic creation process described in the Sarga sections of Puranas.
Avatara of Vishnu, supreme deity, ally of the Pandavas, and teacher of Arjuna.
Third Pandava prince, the archer disciple of Sri Krishna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra.
Eldest Pandava prince, known for his adherence to truth and righteousness.
Second Pandava brother, known for his colossal physical strength.
Fourth Pandava prince, twin of Sahadeva, known for his beauty and expertise with swords.
Fifth Pandava prince, twin of Nakula, renowned for his wisdom and astrological skills.
The princess of Panchala, common wife of the five Pandava brothers.
Great warrior and ally of Duryodhana, son of Surya and Kunti.
The eldest son of Dhritarashtra and leader of the Kaurava faction.
The blind king of Hastinapura, father of the Kauravas.
Half-brother of Dhritarashtra and Pandu, prime minister of Hastinapura, famous for his wisdom.
The grand patriarch of the Kuru dynasty, commander of the Kaurava army.
The royal preceptor and teacher of military arts to both Pandavas and Kauravas.
A family preceptor of the Kurus who fought on the Kaurava side.
King of Hastinapura, younger brother of Dhritarashtra, father of the Pandavas.
The capital city of the Kuru kingdom, over which the war is fought.
The capital city built by the Pandavas in the forest of Khandavaprastha.
The island city ruled by Krishna.
Action and its corresponding consequences.
The great 18-day battle between the Pandavas and Kauravas.
The monumental Sanskrit epic detailing the history of the Kuru dynasty.
The sacred battlefield where the Mahabharata war and Gita dialogue occur.
Liberation from the cycle of samsara.
The spiritual path of love, devotion, and surrender to the Divine.
Prince of Gandhara, maternal uncle of Duryodhana, architect of the game of dice.
The celestial abode of Lord Vishnu.
The divine sage and messenger of the gods.
The benevolent demon king who offered three paces of land to Vamana avatar.
The sacred city of Shiva, center of pilgrimage.
Vedic solar deity of friendship, contracts, and alliances, closely associated with Varuna.
The physical and moral order of the universe that gods and humans must follow.
The oldest of the four Vedas, containing hymns to the deities.
The cosmic giant or primeval being from whose sacrifice the universe was created.
The goddess of dawn, bringing light and life to the world.
Vedic solar deity representing the morning sun, associated with the Gayatri mantra.
The deified sacred plant and drink of immortality offered in Vedic rituals.
The earth goddess, consort of Dyaus Pita.
The storm deities, companions of Indra and sons of Rudra.
A saintly prince, devotee of Vishnu, saved from his demon father Hiranyakashipu.
Cosmic era ruled by a specific Manu, consisting of 71 Mahayugas.
The sacred mountain abode of Lord Shiva.
The Veda of melodies and chants chanted by Udgatri priests.
An aged, pious votaress and pupil of Matanga who waits for Ráma's arrival before ascending to heaven.
The forest region where Ráma dwelt in exile and where he slew the giant Khara and his host.
A sacred grove once inhabited by Saint Matanga and his pupils, where Savarí maintains her hermitage.
The formal alliance between Ráma and Sugríva, witnessed by the sacred fire.
Monarch of Ayodhya and father of Rama, Bharat, Lakshmana, and Shatrughna.
attributes.state: Grief-stricken by the exile of his son

attributes.description: Ruler of Mithila and father of Sita and Urmila.
attributes.legacy: Guardian of the Great Bow of Rudra

attributes.description: A hump-backed slave-born handmaid who plots against Rama.
The resistless power described by Rama as the reason for his sudden change in fortune.
The wild forest where Rama is sentenced to spend 14 years in exile.
Rama strings and snaps the celestial bow of Shiva, winning Sita's hand.
Kaikeyi's demand for Bharat's kingship and Rama's 14-year banishment.
The sky father deity, oldest deity of the Vedic pantheon.
The Veda of prose mantras and ritual formulas.
The Veda of spells, charms, and everyday procedures.
Matriarch of the Kuru dynasty, wife of King Shantanu, mother of Vyasa.
Son of Dronacharya, powerful warrior and survivor of the Kurukshetra War.
The sacred dialogue spoken by Krishna to Arjuna before the Kurukshetra War.
First consort of Shiva, who self-immolated at her father Daksha's sacrifice.
A fierce manifestation of Shiva created to destroy Daksha's sacrifice.
A terrifying manifestation of Shiva associated with destruction and protection.
Sage blessed with eternal youth, saved from Yama by Shiva.
The sacred mountain home of Lord Shiva.
The fourteen cosmic realms of the universe.
The four cosmic eras: Satya, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali.
The creation of the universe by Brahma.
The dissolution of the cosmos.
The churning of the cosmic ocean to obtain Amrita (nectar of immortality).
The sacrificial ritual hosted by Daksha that ended in destruction.
Genre of eighteen major sacred texts detailing cosmology, genealogies, and myths.
The deceased King of Ayodhyá, father of Ráma, Bharat, LakshmaG, and Śatrughna. He died of a broken heart following Ráma's exile.
The chief queen of King Daśaratha and mother of Ráma. She is depicted as a prostrate, lustreless star in her grief.
The chief household priest who manages the transition of power and sends envoys to fetch Bharat.
The King of Kekaya and Bharat's maternal grandfather.
A state of anarchy where no law is known, the strong prey on the weak like fish, and religious/social order collapses.
A prophetic nightmare where Bharat saw his father falling into a lake of mire, drinking oil, and heading south in an ass-drawn chariot.
The process of preserving the king's body in a cask of oil until Bharat's return.
A spirit possessing the strength of a thousand elephants, cursed to be a man-eating monster.
Also known as Siddháshrama; the site of VishGu's penance and Vi[vámitra's sacrifice.
The sacred, mind-born lake of Brahmá located near Mount Kailása.
The gambling match between Yudhishthira and Shakuni which led to the exile.
The childless monarch of Ayodhyá who decrees a great sacrifice to obtain sons.
The holy hermit and son of VibháGdak who guides the specific sacrificial forms of the Ashvamedha.
The second rite begun by Rishya[ring using Atharva texts specifically to win sons for the king.
Another name for the river Yamuná, personified as the Sun-born Goddess.
A virtuous sage whose hermitage Ráma visits and who directs Ráma to Agastya.
A hermit who follows Ráma and explains the mystery of the Panchápsaras lake.
A great votary who created the Panchápsaras lake through his rites and was seduced by five nymphs.
A holy hermit living south of SutíkshGa’s retreat, whose home Ráma visits first.
A demon who took the form of a ram to kill Bráhmans, eventually devoured and destroyed by Agastya.
A fierce demon who spoke Sanskrit to trick Bráhmans; he was consumed to dust by Agastya's glance.
The 'Lake of the Five Nymphs,' created by MáG akarGi, featuring a hidden palace beneath the waters.

Soon as she knew king's fate sharp pangs smote, could not look on lord, torrents poured
Ráma asked Lakshmana bring Ingudí fruit and bark mantle, pay offering due
Lakshmana addressed to bring fruit for offering
Noble mind follower with brothers' aid soothed grief, lent arm to guide lord to holy side
Hero found lovely stream, bent footsteps in bitter sorrow down fair descent
Brother participated in descent with Ráma
Prince came to river shore, holy side, lovely stream ever blossomed
Where stream showed pure shallow, duly shed drops, filled hollowed hand, turned to southern side stretched arm weeping
Offered fresh tribute with brothers' aid, mixed jujube with Ingudí seed on sacred grass, weeping said enjoy cake
Brother aided in offering fresh tribute to father's shade
Prince paid due offerings to parting shade, sacred water to spirits, offering shall aye endure
King lord of kings received offering, tribute to shade, blessed Gods ne'er refuse to eat food children use
Ráma retraced path up mountain, gained cottage door, strained brothers and Sítá to breast
Lady strained to Ráma's breast, cry of weeping rose loud as roar of lions
Brother strained to Ráma's breast, weeping echoed round mountain
Hero's cottage door, sacrificial ground, dwelling
Bharat's army shook with fear hearing weeping, soldiers cried 'Bharat his brother meets again'
Every man left car and wain, ran toward weeping, youthful captains sped to meet lord
Sinless glorious chief found on sacrificial ground, looked on multitude, threw arms about loved ones
Crowd loaded curses on hump-back and queen, cheeks wet eyes dim, ran in fond affection
Person loaded with curses deep and loud by weeping crowd
Saint soul athirst to look on Ráma, placed royal widows in line, traced way behind them
Ladies moving faint and slow saw stream, saw bank's descent, saw funeral offering
Queen with faded cheek weeping eyes spoke mournful tones to Sumitrá and rest
Hero left seat, clasped ladies' lotus feet, they brushed dust from shoulders
Brother drew near royal ladies, paid gentle reverence, received love and tenderness
Lady bent before widows, pressed feet with tears, they embraced her as darling child
Ráma pressed saint's feet with reverential love, took seat near as Indra clasps Heavenly Teacher's feet
Bharat with duteous mind, counselors, peers, citizens, captains sat humbly behind
Each counselor and peer, nobles pressed round hero
Bharat addressed chief: mother made content, government given, yield to thee, people long to see lord
Ráma heard words people approved, pressed by crowd with same request
Steadfast Ráma consoled lamenting prince: mortal still, fate's slave, death inevitable, father won skies
Prince with mournful mood, each ambitious thought controlled, heard steadfast Ráma's words
Good Bharat replied with varied lore: pardon mother's sin, dismiss trespass, I pray with head bent low
Ráma heard Bharat strive to bend will with suppliant head
Earth's lord inexorable still, would keep sire's decree, firmness moved people
Bharat strove to bend will, but steadfast chief kept promise, people wept and approved
Ráma far renowned replied: father swore kingdoms, gave boons, bade me to forest, I keep word
Ráma said: Bharat lord of men reign Ayodhyá, I king of wild domain, thee to umbrella, me to shadow of bough
Bharat urged to take royal might, white umbrella over brow, Shatrughna attend
Virtuous chief of twice-born sages replied in words that virtue's law defied
Ráma heard Jáváli after soothing brother's grief
Ráma son of king, offered tribute to father's shade, keep father's word, father swore kingdoms
Lakshmana knows thy sire is dead, participated in funeral rites
Bharat son of king, told mournful news, father died rich in holy fame, dare not blame aged king
Ráma's moonbright dame, dear consort, faithful wife who follows
Ráma's brother, faithful friend, ever faithful found, mournful, wise
Ráma's dear brother, duteous, noble, steadfast, tamer of foe
Bharat's mother who committed sin, made content, wed by king, saved king's life
Kausalyá's son, queen mother, clasped her lotus feet
Ráma taught: all must waste, death inevitable, ripened fruit falls, life flies as torrents
Ráma said: night flies no returns, death travels with us, lives decay, drifting logs meet and part
Bharat urged: yield government, return to realm, consecration paramount, householder order best
Ráma keeps father's word: king swore kingdoms, gave boons, promise brought to mind, word inviolate
Bharat urged: son born to free sire, Puttra from Put deliverer, funeral rites at Gayá release from pains
Jáváli spoke words defying virtue's law: ties of kindred nothing, alone babe opens eyes alone dies
King enrolled with Gods on high, devout famed, with Gods rolled, gained godlike bliss
Ráma compared to Indra's peer, took seat as Indra clasps Heavenly Teacher's feet
Indra in realms above clasps Heavenly Teacher's feet
King gained blissful home in Brahmá's heavenly home for aye
Ráma back to home go no more, widowed state reft of king, if to town I go
Bharat urged to speed to town, reign over fair town, lord of men
Ráma to DaG ak wood will stray, king of wild domain, silvan world
King's son to free sire from Put's infernal pains through funeral rites
Sítá told tale of Maiden's Choice, how Janak held ceremony, Ráma won hand, princes declined gift
Virtuous-souled matron heard tale, sweet-toned words charmed ear, kissed lady's brow, laced loving arms
Great king father held Maiden's Choice unparalleled, princes declined gift till learned father's mind
Prince won Sítá's maiden hand from princes of land, exalted high above heroic chiefs
Matron spoke: sun sunk from sight, world left to holy Night, leafy thickets sound, hermits return
Saints with pitchers in hand, fresh from bath locks wet bark coats dripping, tend worship fires, smoke ascends
Lady arrayed in heavenly raiment, shone like heavenly Goddess, bowed to matron's feet, turned to meet lord
Saintly dame gave love-gifts: garland of celestial twine, ornaments, robes divine
Hero's heart filled with pride happiness, honored by every pious sage, spent sacred night well content beside darling
Brother's heart filled with pride happiness, Sítá gained honours high mortal dames scarce obtained
When night fled princes said farewell to all hermits, gazed on distant shade, paid lustral rites
Saints addressed pair: monsters fierce and fell dwell in distant forest, feed on blood, rend holy saints, chase or destroy
Valiant hero stood in vast shade of wood, saw hermit settlement, loosed mighty bow-string, drew nearer
Hero in vast shade, mighty forest, deep-tangled wood, wild
Saints endued with keen celestial sight viewed chieftain with joy, came to meet prince/Sítá/Lakshmana, gave glad blessings
Glorious prince received in hermitage, entranced wondering eyes with form stature grace
Pure hermits led guest within cot with leaves overhead, received with highest honor, gave water for feet, brought roots and fruit
Duty-loving hermits reverent hand to hand: king is protector, thou shouldst give protection, we live in thy realm
Prince addressed: king protector maintainer of right, one fourth Indra's essence, whether in town or wood thou art king
Entertained passed night, bade adieu, pierced mighty forest where roamed deer pard bear
Giant passing nature's law, vast as mountain, hideous, wore tiger's hide reeking, carried beasts on iron spear
Deep in thickets with Lakshmana and spouse stood, saw giant, fierce eye fell on three
Giant charged with roar and yell, took trembling Sítá to side, withdrew little space, demanded who are ye
Janak's hapless child scared by fierce speech, trembled for terror as frail plantain in gale
Saw Virádha clasp Sítá in mighty grasp, with pale lips terror dried cried to brother: Kaikeyí's blow falls
Ráma said: now falls blow Kaikeyí meant, cruel soul triumphant, day brings triumph to younger queen
Hero raised cord and arrow, seven arrows flew winged with gold, smote giant's breast purpled like peacock's throat
Fiend unclasped Maithil dame, grasped spear, wild with rage pierced through rushed at brothers
Princely brothers poured fiery flood of arrowy rain on fierce giant
Giant's life unharmed by Brahmá's saving promise, charmed frame
From Ráma's bow two arrows flew cleft massive spear in two, dire as flaming levin
Swift drew sword black as serpent, gathering fury rushed on giant foe
Brother drew sword, rushed fiercely on giant, giant cast arm around held fast
Rover of night upraised them to shoulders, like children clung to neck, bore princes through wood vast as cloud
Viewed with wild affright heroes hurried from sight, tossed arms high, shrieked bitter cry, begged take me
With resistless stroke broke foe's left arm that held him
Swift to smite smashed with heavy hand the right arm
Rushed on crushed and beat with arms fists feet, nerved mighty limb to pound and bray
Saw no arms might slay charmed life, gave counsel dig pit, pressed foot upon giant's breast
Obedient Lakshmana plied spade, dug pit both deep and wide
Prostrate monster with joy heard Ráma's word: I yield overthrown, my name was Tumburu, cursed by Kuvera
Virádha was Tumburu renowned for song, cursed by Kuvera for loving Rambhá, wore hideous shape
Lord's stern decree cursed Tumburu, anger fell for loving Rambhá too well, when Ráma destroys proper shape resume
Holy hermit's cot stands league beyond, sun not more bright, most glorious anchorite
Giant spoke: first under earth throw body, law ordained of old for giants, bodies laid in earth rise to eternal homes
Ráma put foot on neck, threw down mighty form, awful shout gave, sank into grave, spirit fled to heaven
Spirit of Tumburu freed by Ráma's favor, saved to heaven go
Prince won Sítá's maiden hand, truly love exalted above heroic chiefs, moonbright dame, dear spouse
Sítá daughter of royal Janak, bride of Dasharatha's son, maiden hand won
Sister next to Sítá given to be Lakshmana's bride, graced with all gifts
Ráma's brother, minister, nobly brave, faithful companion
Kausalyá blest to be mother of son like Ráma
Lakshmana is Sumitrá's son
Ráma son of king, loss of sire, royal power
Younger queen, Bharat on throne, greedy eyes, dark intent
Virtuous Ráma fair as Soma in evening air
Ráma peer of Indra, might vies with Indra's own, king bears one fourth of Indra's essence
Brahmá's grace gave boon charmed frame weapon never pierce, Brahmá's saving promise
Kuvera's stern decree cursed Tumburu, angry God whose wrath fell
Tumburu loving Rambhá's charms too well caused Kuvera's curse
Saints abode in circle where coats of bark hung, holy grass bestrewed
Pure dwellers in shade, old reverend sires, delightful grove like Brahmá's sphere
Holy hermit's cot stands league beyond in Dandaka
Great Virádha day by day through deep-tangled wood stray
Ráma is son of Dasharatha
Ráma fills Kausalyá's soul with joy as her son
Lakshmana is son of Dasharatha
Bharat is son of Dasharatha
Shatrughna is son of Dasharatha

Yudhájit is son of Kekaya lord
Sítá is daughter of Janak
