Two daughters are children of Kushadhwaj
Janak and Kushadhwaj are brothers, Janak elder
Ráma to marry Sítá, won her by breaking bow
Lakshmana to marry Urmilá
Bharat to marry one daughter of Kushadhwaj
Shatrughna to marry one daughter of Kushadhwaj
Ráma broke heavenly bow in two
Ráma raised, drew string, broke mighty bow
Janak possessed bow as treasure from ages
Janak vowed daughter to hero who could string bow
Dasharatha traveled to Videha with priests and host
Dasharatha reached and rested at Mithilá
Janak greeted Dasharatha as guest, allied houses through marriage
Kushadhwaj summoned and came from Sánkáshyá
Kushadhwaj rules in Sánkáshyá
Yudhájit came to see nephew Bharat
Vishvámitra guarded and led Ráma
Vishvámitra guarded and led Lakshmana
Vasishtha protects Ikshváku line, speaks for Dasharatha
Vasishtha claimed four daughters for four princes
Shatánanda is chaplain and priest of Janak, counselor
Brahmá had son Maríchi
Kashyapa is son of Maríchi
Vivasvat is son of Kashyapa
Manu is son of Vivasvat
Manu begot Ikshváku, gave life to mortals
Kukshi is son of Ikshváku
Purushádak is son of Raghu
Aja is son of Nábhág
Dasharatha is glorious son of Aja
Kálindi is widowed queen of Asit
Kálindi bore Sagar conceived by Chyavan
Chyavan is spiritual father of Sagar through boon
Rival wife mixed poison to prevent Kálindi's conception
Asit fought fierce warfare, forced to flee to Himálaya
Asit fled to Himálaya and died there
Nimi's son and heir was Mithi
Janak (first) is son of Mithi
Hrasvaromá is father of Janak
Hrasvaromá is father of Kushadhwaj
Sudhanvá threatened siege, fought Janak
Janak slew Sudhanvá with single hand
Sudhanvá demanded Shiva's bow
Sudhanvá demanded Sítá
Haihayas were rival kings fighting Asit
Dasharatha gave four hundred thousand cows
Dasharatha distributed gift of cows as dowry
Janak agreed to marry daughters to four princes
Janak planned nuptials for third night in Phálguni
Ráma with Lakshmana and Sumantra paid evening worship, lay on grass bed
Lakshmana brought sacred grass, spread bed, bathed feet, kept vigil all night as sentinel
Faithful Sítá lay reclining by Ráma on pleasant bed
Charioteer one of three who paid evening worship, kept watch with followers
Prince spent night with Sítá under tree that lent shade
Brother kept great bow strung, hand gloved, arm braced, quivers hung, watched as sentinel
Prince with friends and peers went to tree, saw bed Ráma pressed, lamented
Queen mother with Sumitrá came to see bed
Queen came with Kausalyá to see site
Prince beheld tumbled grass, particles of gold, silken threads, lamented sudden fall
From this hour nights on earth/grass, eat fruit/roots, wear bark/matted hair, remain for Ráma's term
Son of Raghu lay on Gangá's bank till break of day, woke with earliest light
Brother addressed by Ráma to rise, said remembering Ráma sleepless lie
Nishádas' king came with kindly questioning about night and army's ease
Ráma's faithful slave answered king: night sweetly passed, highly honored
King swift to do bidding, sped to town, told kinsmen, five hundred boats ready moored
Prince swiftly sprang on board with Shatrughna
Famous lord boarded with Bharat
Queen with many royal dames came to boats
Queen came with Kausalyá and royal dames
Household priest went first in place with elders and Bráhman race
Prince crossed pure Gangá's wave with army's help
Army in auspicious hour stood in famous wood, prince addressed weary men
Prince arrived at famous wood after crossing Gangá
Prince saw dwelling league away, left multitude, went on foot, Vasishtha walked before
Good saint walked before Bharat to hermitage
Saint austere saw Vasishtha drawing near, gave grace-gift, fruit, questioned
Prince paid reverence due, questioned about Ayodhyá, received welcome
Glorious hermit dwelling in holy shade
Saint asked why Bharat came, if to harm Ráma, love of Ráma moved breast
Prince with sobbing voice tearful eye sadly replied: lost if heart attainted, ne'er such crime proceed
Hermit asked if Bharat would harm Ráma, enjoy realm without thorn
Hermit pressed by Vasishtha replied graciously: worthy deed, true son, granted prayer
Saint pressed hermit along with gathered priests
Hermit told Bharat: where Chitrakúta's heights arise thy brother's dwelling lies
Hermit prayed to Vishvakarma, world-guards, streams, minstrels, Apsarases
God who forms all heard call to aid hospitable feast
Lord at head of three who guard worlds called to hermit's feast
One of three world-guards called by hermit
One of three world-guards called
Lord called, garden from Northern Kuru summoned
Gandharva king called to lead celestial strain
Bright nymphs called with all their train
Lord of Gods came in manifest semblance
Bright ones danced, twenty thousand from Nandan came
Gandharva king with Nárad/Gopa/Sutanu ravished Bharat with song
Fairest maid danced to Bharat with graceful ease
God's art raised structures: five leagues smooth ground, fruit trees, mansions, palace
Art created amazing sights troops saw: level ground, trees, streams, mansions, palace high
Lord's garden from Northern Kuru summoned, wood rich in delights glorious
Prince drew near palace, passed within fair abode, sat on throne with chouri
Priest led way, councillors followed delighted and amazed
Officers sat by degrees from chief to least
Lord sent twenty thousand nymphs divine with celestial ornament
Gandharva with Nárad/Gopa/Sutanu ravished Bharat with song
Fairest maid danced with graceful ease
Prince's army spent night in hermit's grove in joy and merriment
Army delirious with delight, soldiers cried ne'er return to Ayodhyá, heaven for bliss
Ráma's dear consort, faithful wife, lay reclining by him
Ráma's brother who guards, tamer of foe, kept vigil for him
Bharat is Ráma's faithful slave, seeks to lead him to royal seat, win lordly hero's grace
Shatrughna is brother by Bharat's side, lion-mettled, will share exile
Kaikeyí is Bharat's mother whose guilty words he denies approving
Ráma is Kausalyá's son, line's delight
Ráma is son of royal sire, heir banished by father
Hermit knows where Ráma dwells at Chitrakúta with brother and wife
Household priest walked before Bharat, led councillors
Hermit saw good Vasishtha drawing near, was aware Dasharatha's son there
Where heights arise thy brother's dwelling lies
Prince roams in banishment in wilderness
Hermit's dwelling in famous wood
King of Nishádas whose town provided boats
Lord's garden lies in Northern Kuru, happy land in remote north
Twenty thousand maids came from Nandan's blissful shades
Sweetest nymphs stand by Indra's hand
Nymphs stand by Brahmá's hand
Ráma is described as embodying divine power comparable to VishGu throughout the epic.
Ráma is the eldest and most beloved son of King Dasharatha.
Ráma is the son of Queen Kaushalya who blesses him before exile.
Sítá is Ráma's devoted wife who accompanies him into exile.
LakshmaG is Ráma's younger brother and constant companion in exile.
Ráma is the prince of Ayodhyá who departs from and will eventually return to this city.
Ráma, Sítá, and LakshmaG spend 14 years in exile in the DaGak forest.
Ráma willingly accepts the 14-year exile to uphold his father's sworn word.
Ráma departs Ayodhyá in Sumantra's chariot with Sítá and LakshmaG.
Ráma halts under the Ingudí tree on the Gangá bank on the first night of exile.
Ráma visits sage Bharadvája at the confluence of Gangá and Yamuná.
Sítá insists on accompanying Ráma into the forest despite his objections.
Sítá departs Ayodhyá with Ráma in Sumantra's chariot.
Sítá accompanies Ráma into the DaGak forest during the exile.
LakshmaG voluntarily accompanies Ráma as his devoted protector during exile.
LakshmaG departs with Ráma and Sítá from Ayodhyá.
LakshmaG is the son of Queen Sumitrá, who blesses him to serve Ráma faithfully.
LakshmaG stands guard through the night at the Ingudí tree while Ráma sleeps.
Kaikeyí is one of Dasharatha's queens whose boon leads to Ráma's exile.
Kaushalya is Dasharatha's senior queen and mother of Ráma.
Dasharatha is the King of Ayodhyá who rules Ko[ala from this capital.
Dasharatha dies of grief after sending Ráma into exile.
Dasharatha accidentally killed the hermit's son on the Sarju bank in his youth while hunting by sound.
Dasharatha repeatedly speaks of Ráma in grief and love after the exile.
Kaikeyí demands Ráma's banishment as one of her two boons from Dasharatha.
Kaikeyí resides in the palace of Ayodhyá and schemes within its walls.
Kaushalya performs elaborate blessing rites for Ráma before his departure.
Kaushalya blesses Ráma and entreats him to take Sítá safely to the forest.
Kaushalya counsels Sítá on the duty of a devoted wife before departure.
Sumitrá instructs LakshmaG to treat Ráma as his lord and Sítá as his mother.
Sumantra drives the royal chariot carrying Ráma, Sítá, and LakshmaG out of Ayodhyá.
Sumantra returns alone to Ayodhyá bearing Ráma's parting messages.
Sumantra returns to Ayodhyá after leaving Ráma at the forest.
Guha, the Nisháda chief, comes to welcome Ráma at the Gangá bank and keeps night watch.
Guha is Ráma's dear friend of Nisháda lineage and sovereign lord of the Nisháda region.
Guha's kingdom lies along the Gangá bank near the Ingudí tree.
Vaishtha presides over the assembly of sages and ministers after the king's death.
Vaishtha orders the dispatch of fast messengers to summon Bharat.
Bharat stays in Rájagriha with his maternal grandfather during Ráma's exile.
Bharat is Ráma's younger brother by Kaikeyí who reveres Ráma and refuses the throne.
Zatrughna accompanies Bharat at Rájagriha during the exile period.
Bharadvája's hermitage is located near the confluence of Gangá and Yamuná at Prayag.
Bharadvája receives Ráma and LakshmaG at his hermitage near Prayag.
Nárad describes Ráma's divine virtues to Válmíki.
Indra is invoked in blessings comparing Ráma's victories to Indra's slaying of Vritra.
Dharma is the guiding principle behind all of Ráma's decisions including acceptance of exile.
Ráma's filial duty to obey his father drives his willingness to accept exile.
Dasharatha's sworn oath to Kaikeyí compels him to send Ráma into exile against his will.
The assembly debates the necessity of kingship for Ayodhyá's welfare after Dasharatha's death.
The Rámáyan is the sacred text that narrates all events of Ráma's life and exile.
This canto records the ritual blessing Kaushalya performs for Ráma before his departure.
This canto narrates how Sítá convinces Ráma to let her accompany him into the forest.
This canto records the formal leave-taking from Ayodhyá.
This canto describes Ráma's first night halt at the Gangá bank.

relationships.source: The Halt Under The Ingudí (Canto L)

attributes.context: This canto captures LakshmaG's emotional lament during the night watch at the Gangá bank.
This canto narrates Dasharatha's confession of accidentally killing the hermit's son.
This canto presents the Bráhmans' discourse on why a kingless land is doomed.
This canto records Vaishtha's dispatch of envoys to summon Bharat.
This canto describes Ráma's arrival at Bharadvája's hermitage near Prayag.
Ráma is son, touches father's feet, fulfills father's word as paramount duty, father is deity on earth
Ráma is matchless son, hero boy, darling of soul, tender and affectionate, loves to wait on mother
Ráma is husband of large-eyed wife, Sítá possessed whole of his heart and soul
Ráma touched Kaikeyí's feet, more dutiful to her than own mother, addressed as dear Queen
Lakshmana kept close with fraternal care, followed behind, brother's peer in princely virtues

Kausalyá is queen, royal dame, consort
Kaikeyí is queen who saved king's life, earned two boons, sits with him
Sítá attended lord to door, implored blessings, prayed for consecration success
Sítá invoked Thunder-wielder to guard Ráma in east
Sítá invoked Yáma to befriend south
Sítá invoked Varuna to defend west
Sítá invoked Kuvera to hold north with protection

Sovereign in palace bright, weeping for son, pale, faint, sorrow-worn

Husband and wife; Ráma's primary quest is to recover Sítá after her abduction.
Brothers; LakshmaG is Ráma's devoted companion and protector throughout their exile.
RávaG abducts Sítá from Janasthán and imprisons her in his city of Lanká.
Márícha acts as RávaG's accomplice, using magic to lure Ráma away and facilitate the abduction.
Jatáyus fights RávaG to prevent him from carrying Sítá away.
The two form a 'League' of mutual aid to recover their lost wives and kingdoms.
Hanumán is Sugríva's faithful counselor and envoy.
Brothers and enemies; Báli expelled Sugríva and took his wife, leading Sugríva to seek Ráma's help.
After being slain by Ráma, Kabandha's spirit advises Ráma to seek Sugríva's alliance.
Savarí welcomes Ráma to Matanga's wood and honors him as her guest.
Sítá is held captive in the A[oka garden within the city of Lanká.
Sugríva resides on Mount Rishyamúka as a fugitive from his brother Báli.
Hanumán is the son of the God of Wind.
Báli is the son of the God Indra.
